serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of beef tendon in chili sate sauce is approximately 300 g, typical of a Vietnamese-style stir-fry portion served in a Singaporean food court or restaurant context.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, variety meats and by-products, cooked (FDC ID 174033); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient composition data for stir-fried beef dishes
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for beef tendon stir-fried in sate chili sauce exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef variety meats cooked (FDC ID 174033) combined with HPB Singapore stir-fried beef dish analogues. Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as the primary numeric basis given their internal consistency and plausibility against approved-source analogues. Energy 150 kcal/100g, protein 11.67g, fat 9.33g, carbohydrate 5.0g are consistent with a collagen-rich braised-then-stir-fried beef product with sauce. All values documented as estimated. GrabFood is not used as a primary nutrient source; it is cited only as the origin of the input record.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef tendon stir-fried in sate chili sauce. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous category for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes with sauce, consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed reference values. Carbohydrate per 100g is 5.0g. GL per serving = (35 × 5.0 × 3.0) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.3. This is consistent with low-GI, low-GL classification for a protein-dominant dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100g from fish sauce and sate chili sauce; a 300g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150g or less, request reduced fish sauce and sate chili sauce, and pair the dish with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or sweet potato to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5.0g/100g) and low sugar (1.67g/100g); high protein (11.67g/100g) from collagen-rich beef tendon slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose response; estimated GI of 35 places this dish in the low-GI category.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef tendon in chili sate sauce is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (150–200g), provided the sate sauce is requested in reduced quantity to limit added sugars and sodium; monitor cumulative carbohydrate intake from accompanying rice or noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.33g/100g and trans fat 0.17g/100g from cooking oil and sate sauce; sodium 400mg/100g elevates cardiovascular risk; however, collagen-rich beef tendon is relatively lower in saturated fat than muscle meat cuts.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions (100–150g), choose preparations with minimal added oil, request reduced sate sauce to lower saturated fat and sodium, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium or high-fat dishes in the same meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a red meat source of arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) that can amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); chili (nightshade) may trigger joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat frequency, monitor personal response to chili (nightshade), and request reduced fish sauce to lower sodium-related tissue swelling; consider substituting with a leaner protein source on high-inflammation days.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Tendon in Chili Sate Sauce (Vietnamese: Gân Bò Xào Sa Tế; Chinese: 沙爹辣酱炒牛筋) is a Vietnamese stir-fried dish featuring slow-braised beef tendon that is then wok-tossed in a bold, aromatic sate chili sauce. Beef tendon is a collagen-rich connective tissue cut that becomes gelatinous and tender after prolonged braising; it has a soft, slightly chewy texture with a rich, savory mouthfeel. The sate sauce (sa tế) is a Southeast Asian chili-lemongrass paste typically made from dried chili, lemongrass, shallots, garlic, and shrimp paste or fish sauce, delivering deep umami heat with fragrant citrus-herbal notes. The dish is commonly served as a main protein component alongside steamed rice or rice noodles in Vietnamese restaurants and food courts across Singapore. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 150 kcal per 100g, with 11.67g protein, 9.33g fat, and only 5.0g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is elevated at 400mg/100g due to fish sauce and sate chili sauce, which is a key clinical concern for hypertensive individuals. The estimated glycemic index is 35 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 300g serving is approximately 5.3 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and contains fish allergens from fish sauce. It is a culturally significant Vietnamese comfort dish that has been widely adopted in the Singaporean hawker and restaurant scene.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for beef tendon stir-fried in sate chili sauce exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are retained from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, original confidence 0.7) and cross-validated for plausibility against USDA beef tendon and HPB stir-fried beef analogues. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore set to low.

Nutrient values sourced from input record (ai_generated, grabfood provenance, confidence 0.7) and validated for plausibility against USDA FoodData Central beef variety meats cooked (FDC ID 174033) and HPB Singapore stir-fried beef dish data. GrabFood is not used as a primary nutrient source. 4.
